sentence: Local Authorities, Powers of—Freedom of Contract, proposed, 393, 401, 407, 412, 418, 425, 428, 432, 433.
haiku_v2_rationale: This is an index entry listing page numbers for discussion of "Freedom of Contract" as a proposed power for local authorities; it makes no substantive claim about liberty itself.

sentence: She has won this freedom.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence uses bare "freedom" without specifying what kind of freedom or whether it is freedom from interference or freedom to act; insufficient context to determine whether this refers to negative or positive liberty.

sentence: Where these students are entrusted with freedom it is not abused, but in the very exceptional case.
haiku_v2_rationale: Students are entrusted with freedom (non-interference/autonomous action), and the sentence states this freedom is not abused except rarely—affirming their capacity to exercise self-direction responsibly.

sentence: I am aware, of course, that concern about freedom of information derives in some cases more from genuine conviction than simple political opportunism.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom of information is a classic negative liberty: it concerns freedom from restrictions on access to and dissemination of information, analogous to freedom of speech or press.

sentence: The Catalonian parliamentarians have the right to freedom of expression when they debate the question of independence, which they have done, so I do not really see why the hon.
haiku_v2_rationale: Catalonian parliamentarians' freedom of expression—freedom from interference with their ability to debate independence—is a negative liberty claim.

sentence: Nobody in this House is more in sympathy with the desire for self-government and freedom than those who are supporting this Motion.
haiku_v2_rationale: The phrase "freedom" is invoked as a rhetorical ideal opposed to constraint, paired with "self-government" as a positive value to be championed—the freedom being discussed is the capacity of a people/nation to govern itself, which is positive liberty or empowerment.

sentence: Many have been led to believe that the Bill will contain stronger and better methods of safeguarding the liberty of the individual.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence discusses safeguarding liberty of the individual (protection from interference), which is negative liberty—freedom from violation or constraint of personal autonomy.

sentence: Those principles include our belief in free markets, our belief in private enterprise and in personal freedom and personal responsibility and the reintroduction of proper deterrence and punishment into the law and order system.
haiku_v2_rationale: Personal freedom here names freedom from interference with individual choice and action (in contrast to state control of markets and enterprise), aligned with negative liberty doctrines.

sentence: and that instead of tearing each other to pieces with a waste of blood, such as few wars have occasioned, some compromise could have been effected, favourable at once to rational principles of liberty, and to the peace of the old world and the new!
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker invokes "rational principles of liberty" as an ideal standing opposed to warfare and bloodshed—a rhetorical invocation of liberty as freedom from tyranny and oppression, characteristic of negative liberty discourse in the context of peace and national self-determination.

sentence: and his Ministers thought of the meetings which shouted for "Wilkes and Liberty"?
haiku_v2_rationale: "Wilkes and Liberty" is a rhetorical invocation of liberty as a political cause and rallying cry against perceived tyranny and government oppression, referencing freedom from governmental interference.

sentence: The rights and the freedoms of, and our responsibilities in this country to, the people of Hong Kong should not be sacrificed on the altar of bankers’ bonuses.
haiku_v2_rationale: Rights and freedoms of Hong Kong people are being protected against interference/sacrifice for economic interests (bankers' bonuses), invoking negative liberty—freedom from external constraint or infringement.

sentence: With regard to the circulation of Bank paper, he would take the liberty of making one remark;
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker is using "took the liberty of" as a parliamentary courtesy expression meaning "may I proceed to" or "allow me to"; this is procedural language, not a substantive claim about freedom as a value.

sentence: The laws of arrest mark the legal balance between civil liberty and compulsory powers for the institution of a criminal process.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence discusses civil liberty (freedom from arbitrary arrest and detention interference) balanced against the state's compulsory power to enforce criminal process—a classic negative liberty framing about protection from state interference in personal detention.

sentence: They are such as I think are conducive to the general good, and the liberty and happiness of my fellow-subjects.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence invokes "liberty" as a general good to be promoted for the speaker's fellow-subjects, without specifying what is being freed or from-to what—the liberty is bare and rhetorical.

sentence: It used to be the characteristic policy of England throughout Europe to aid the cause of liberty and independence—to cherish the feelings of patriotism and love of freedom, to restrain the strong, to assist the weak, to resist the encroachments of despotism, and to afford a refuge to the destitute and oppressed.
haiku_v2_rationale: England's historical policy of aiding liberty and independence and resisting despotism describes freedom from domination, oppression, and encroachment by stronger powers—a freedom from interference and subjugation.

sentence: That Conference passed a resolution, he thought with absolute unanimity, urging that liberty should be accorded to the colonies to make fiscal arrangements without reference to any foreign Power.
haiku_v2_rationale: Colonies are freed to make fiscal arrangements independently, without external interference—empowering self-governance in fiscal policy, which is an instance of positive liberty (capacity to govern oneself).

sentence: This whole area is one where we have to ensure that the liberal values, to support freedom but not the freedom to harm others, that we apply through legislation to many other  parts of our lives are brought to bear on the online world as well.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker is discussing constraints on freedom (specifically "freedom to harm others") balanced against liberal values that support freedom more broadly, emphasizing the negative liberty principle of freedom from harm and interference, applied through legislation.

sentence: He read it before it was in print, and had come to the conclusion it was the only way by which they could secure the liberty of the subject and the chance of expressing views which happened to be hostile to those entertained by either the majority or the minority of the audience.
haiku_v2_rationale: The liberty of the subject to be free from constraint on expressing views (especially those hostile to majority or minority opinion); freedom of expression from interference.

sentence: The oil industries are glad to feel that until last year there was comparative freedom from strikes.
haiku_v2_rationale: The oil industries have freedom from strikes — being freed from the interference of strike actions.

sentence: Throughout the realm of human rights, the concepts of personal liberty and freedom which all hon.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence invokes "personal liberty and freedom" as abstract values within human rights discourse, without specifying what is being freed or from-or-to what. The passage is incomplete, making the specific object of freedom unclear.

sentence:
I put it to you, Sir, as a question of order, whether he is at liberty to make that statement
 330 
without announcing that the Government intend to move to rescind the Resolution for the appointment of the Committee.
haiku_v2_rationale: This is parliamentary procedure language: "at liberty to" means "permitted by rules of order to"; it is not a substantive claim about liberty-as-a-value but rather whether standing rules allow the statement.

sentence: I said then that the Good Friday-Belfast agreement was not just about Northern Ireland, Ireland or a border, but the freedom of movement of people across these islands and the deep roots we have.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom of movement is freedom from restrictions on crossing borders/boundaries between Ireland and Northern Ireland—a classic negative liberty protecting from interference with mobility.

sentence: We are desirous of extending in every possible way every possible freedom to trade that we can, consistently with a due regard to the interests of the revenue and the interest of the fair dealer.
haiku_v2_rationale: Traders are freed from interference/restriction to engage in trade, consistently with revenue and fair dealing constraints.

sentence: Will that man be disposed to look kindly on the class legislation that thus abridges his liberty?
haiku_v2_rationale: A man is being restricted ("abridged") in his liberty by class legislation; the concern is freedom from governmental interference that selectively disadvantages a particular class.

sentence: We are now getting a very clear idea of freedom.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence is a bare invocation of "freedom" without specifying what is being freed, from what interference, or to what capacity. No substantive claim about the nature or value of liberty is made.

sentence: The Duke of Edinburgh Award gave them the freedom and inspiration to do something.
haiku_v2_rationale: Young people are freed to take action and achieve their potential through the enabling capacity/empowerment provided by the Award scheme.

sentence: Writing on the 6th of May, 1856, one of the men still living who were responsible for that war pointed out the future danger of an "encroachment upon and absorption of Turkey by Russia," adding that "such a danger to the peace, liberty, and privileges of all Europe we were bound to resist by every means in our power.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence invokes "liberty" as a value threatened by Russian expansion, opposing encroachment that would endanger European political independence and security — a rhetorical invocation of freedom from foreign domination.

sentence: You have equally vocal throughout the same countries a passionate desire for freedom and for justice.
haiku_v2_rationale: A passionate desire for "freedom" invoked as a general political aspiration alongside justice; the object and direction of freedom are not specified—unclear what is being freed or from-or-to what.

sentence: If it is against the doctrine, why not have freedom of conscience as well?
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence invokes "freedom of conscience" as a named civil liberty—freedom from interference with one's conscience or belief—opposing it to a doctrine that would restrict it.

sentence: When we interfered by the Poor Law in Ireland, we did not interfere with the freedom of contract.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom of contract (the freedom from state interference with private contractual arrangements) was not restricted by Poor Law intervention in Ireland.

sentence: They presumed him to have attained a degree of civilization from which he was yet far removed, that he had acquired those habits which would render him an industrious labourer if he were free, and that he regarded freedom with the same feeling as they did, instead of considering it, as he really did, in no other light than as a total cessation from any employment.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom is being characterized as absence of constraint on employment/labor — freedom from imposed work. The contrast drawn is between freedom-as-non-interference with labor and the speaker's assumption that the man would value freedom to engage in industrious work. The object of freedom is release from mandatory employment, a negative liberty concern.

sentence: Since the Prime Minister has often declared support for open government, may I ask the Leader of the House when the Government will bring forward their Freedom of Information Bill?
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom of information is a freedom from governmental interference with access to official records and data—a classic negative liberty claim that citizens/press should not be prevented from obtaining public information.

sentence: Their argument was that for many years the Business of the House had been increasing, and the conclusion they drew was that instead of maintaining the liberty of the subject in proportion to the increase of Business, the facilities for control should be diminished.
haiku_v2_rationale: Liberty of the subject (citizens) is being defended against diminishment of facilities for control—negative liberty from state/parliamentary interference with individual rights and oversight.

sentence: In the Second World War he became Prime Minister again and led his country into the fight for democracy and freedom.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence invokes "freedom" as a rhetorical cause opposed to the tyranny of Nazism in the Second World War — this is freedom from oppression and foreign domination, a classic negative liberty framing.

sentence: Baronet and the other noted apostles of human liberty who support it, have been longing for a funeral that has not taken place.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker invokes "human liberty" as a cause or value that certain apostles support, opposing it to something undesirable; this is a rhetorical invocation of liberty as freedom from constraint or oppression.

sentence: and she said that she felt sure that the system of Government regulation of vice and examination of women is contrary not only to the rights of woman, but to the general liberty of the State.
haiku_v2_rationale: The system of government regulation and examination of women is being condemned as contrary to liberty understood as freedom from state interference in personal/bodily autonomy and intimate matters.

sentence:
Lord Stowell
 1696 
laid down the rule which prevailed in his time in Doctors' Commons on the subject in these words—
 "This Court considers a private separation as an illegal contract, implying a dereliction of stipulated duties, which the parties are not at liberty to desert.
haiku_v2_rationale: The object of freedom is the capacity to make or enforce a private separation contract; the Court denies this freedom, holding that parties are not at liberty to desert their stipulated marital duties—a negative liberty claim about what persons cannot be freed from doing.

sentence: Sir, it has appeared to us that we are at liberty to pursue one or other of three courses with respect to the institution of Maynooth.
haiku_v2_rationale: This is a procedural/parliamentary usage: the speaker asserts that the body is "at liberty to pursue one or other of three courses" — meaning they have the ability or permission to proceed with a decision. This is not making a substantive claim about liberty-as-a-value but rather using "at liberty" in the procedural sense.

sentence: Anyone who is interested in the liberty of the individual and the development of rights knows the posisition.
haiku_v2_rationale: The individual is freed from interference with their personal liberty and rights; the sentence invokes liberty as a cause/value opposed to oppression, making it negative liberty.

sentence: Her whole life seemed to be a series of acts of charity—here, interceding with the Protestant masters or mistresses of some of the Roman Catholic witnesses at her trial, that they might have liberty of fasting or attending mass;
haiku_v2_rationale: The witnesses are freed from interference with their capacity to fast and attend mass—this is freedom of religious practice and conscience.

sentence: Here again, it may be asked, what degree of freedom will there be in these elections?
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence asks about the degree of non-interference or procedural openness available within electoral processes—specifically, what scope for genuine choice and lack of constraint exists in elections.

sentence:
Industrial civil servants, numbering nearly 400,000, are already allowed a very considerable measure of freedom as regards political


and civic activities;
haiku_v2_rationale: Industrial civil servants are allowed freedom regarding political and civic activities—freedom from interference or restriction on their political participation and civic engagement.

sentence: Now, you must excuse my taking the liberty of reminding you that there is not a leading man on either front bench that  The Times,  guided with such wonderful ability, and wielding such a terrific power, has not by turns lauded and calumniated, nattered and vilified.
haiku_v2_rationale: Procedural parliamentary language: "took the liberty of" is a formulaic expression meaning "took the freedom/occasion to" in the sense of initiating an action within debate, not a substantive claim about liberty as freedom from interference or empowered capacity.

sentence: Those schools were usually in private ownership, and not subject to any trust, and the private owner was at liberty to do what he liked with his school.
haiku_v2_rationale: The private owner is freed from constraint/regulation to do what he wishes with his school—a claim about freedom from interference with property and business control.

sentence: Notwithstanding all taunts, Her Majesty's Government would continue to carry out the policy which was contained in the Anglo-Turkish Convention, which he believed in his heart would do much to increase the power of England, to promote liberty in the East, and to secure our position alike in Asia and in Europe.
haiku_v2_rationale: Liberty in the East is being promoted through government policy and the Anglo-Turkish Convention; the object is "liberty" (unspecified groups/peoples in the East) being freed from some form of external constraint or oppression, aligning with negative liberty against tyranny.

sentence:
In 1941, as it was about to enter a world war and face enormous world pressure, Franklin Roosevelt said:
 In the future days which we seek to make secure we look forward to a world founded on four essential human freedoms.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence invokes abstract "human freedoms" as ideals to secure in a future world — a broad rhetorical claim about liberty as a value to be protected and realized, without specifying freedoms from or capacities to do any particular thing.

sentence: There have been two great crimes perpetrated against liberty.
haiku_v2_rationale: Liberty itself is invoked as a value under attack; the speaker identifies crimes against it, but does not specify what freedom (from or to what) is at stake—the sentence is a rhetorical declaration without substantive content about what is being freed.

sentence: It is not freedom for the local education authorities;
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence asserts that something (unspecified in this fragment) is NOT freedom for local education authorities to do or possess—but the sentence is incomplete and does not specify what capacity or action is being denied or what the speaker is contrasting with.

sentence: To echo the sentiments he told this House, it is the contribution we make in the belief in the values that British people have fought and died for — values of democracy, equality and freedom, fairness and tolerance — that make us British.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence invokes "freedom" as an abstract British value in opposition to tyranny and oppression (contrasted with "democracy, equality, fairness and tolerance"), treating it as a cause British people have "fought and died for"—this is a rhetorical invocation of negative liberty (freedom from interference and oppression).

sentence: Was it possible to restrain the language of men and the freedom of the people to this extent?
haiku_v2_rationale: The freedom being invoked is freedom of the people (from restraint of language and expression); the sentence opposes state interference with this freedom.

sentence: The theological colleges, through the Bishop of Gloucester, who knows them, of course, extremely well, roundly say that they will not remain in the University if these regulations controlling their freedom are enforced.
haiku_v2_rationale: The theological colleges' freedom from regulatory controls is being constrained; they oppose regulations that interfere with their existing liberty of action.

sentence: Friends on this side have spoken most eloquently about this erosion of liberty and the need to move most carefully and with considerable forethought in a wider inquiry.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence invokes "erosion of liberty" as a general concern opposed to state overreach, in a parliamentary context debating the scope of inquiry. This is a rhetorical invocation of liberty as freedom from unwanted interference or encroachment.

sentence: Before, however, I come to that part of the subject to which the amendment specially refers, I will take the liberty of adverting to some observations that have fallen from the Mover and Seconder of the Address.
haiku_v2_rationale: The phrase "take the liberty of adverting to" is a parliamentary procedural idiom meaning "permit myself to mention"; it does not claim a value about freedom from interference or empowerment to act.

sentence: I think it is fair to say that there has been some hesitation in the past in our embracing the issue of persecution of Christians — whether from a misguided concern about our history and the role of missionaries, I do not know — but now is the time when we have to put all that behind us and say that freedom of religious belief is an essential and indivisible part of freedom, full stop.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom of religious belief is freedom from interference with one's choice of religion and conscience — a classic negative liberty protecting against state or social persecution.

sentence: We are seeking to create a unity of Government, managers and workers which will bring our nation once again, and soon, I hope, to economic freedom and prosperity.
haiku_v2_rationale: The nation is being freed to achieve economic self-sufficiency and prosperity through unified governance, management, and labor cooperation—an empowerment to flourish economically rather than escape from external constraint.

sentence: All these people have had four or five months of liberty and equality and fraternisation, which always follows any form of revolution.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker refers to "liberty" as a social condition experienced by people following revolution—freedom from previous constraints or oppression—in a rhetorical invocation of liberty as a value opposed to the prior regime.

sentence: However freedom of information requests are dealt with, it is still important that as well as having tough rules and robust audit, we have a scheme to put in the public domain, without its having been asked for, information about what all Members are spending every year, and on what.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom of information requests concerns freedom from interference with access to information; the scheme to proactively publish spending data amplifies this by removing barriers to obtaining government transparency information.

sentence: So far as the question of expenses is concerned, there is nothing in it whatever, nor is there anything in the suggestion which is uncharitably made, and those of us who believe in maintaining the liberty of the subject and object to these statutory tribunals, see no actual prospect of more litigation than exists under the ordinary law.
haiku_v2_rationale: The liberty being defended is the liberty of the subject (citizens) from interference by statutory tribunals—a freedom from a specific form of legal constraint or state intrusion into judicial processes. This invokes negative liberty as a value opposed to expanded state power.

sentence: To what extent should the Government be the arbiters of fate for the individual, and to what extent should the Government interfere with the liberty of people in such matters as this?
haiku_v2_rationale: Individuals are being freed from interference by government in matters of personal choice; the sentence opposes government intervention in personal affairs as a constraint on liberty.

sentence: She will also be well aware that there is a long-standing debate in Northern Ireland about the freedom to set corporation tax rates, which would involve a different arrangement from the one that we have now.
haiku_v2_rationale: A region (Northern Ireland) is being freed to set its own corporation tax rates — this is an empowerment to govern itself with respect to tax policy, a capacity to act autonomously in fiscal matters.

sentence:
The Bill is about the granting of freedom and independence to over 5 million people, the majority of whom have been deprived of freedom, rights and

opportunities for generations.
haiku_v2_rationale: The Bill grants freedom and independence to a people who have been deprived of freedom and rights — this is about empowering a colonized or subjugated population to govern themselves and enjoy self-determination, a capacity/empowerment to act as a free nation.

sentence: The simple fact was, that success no longer beamed so decisively on the cause of liberty in the Peninsula.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker invokes "liberty" as a cause opposed to some opposing force (presumably tyranny or foreign domination) in the Peninsula; freedom from external interference/oppression is what is at stake.

sentence: The Prime Minister also said that our democracy in our Westminster Parliament was the root of our freedom of choice — that was the essence of what he was saying.
haiku_v2_rationale: Westminster Parliament enables freedom of choice; parliament is framed as the source of the capacity to choose.

sentence: and he, as an Irishman, declared that it was in the interests of the freedom and peace of his country that this Act should remain in force, and that it should be in the power of any Government to put its provisions, when the necessity arose into operation.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker invokes "freedom of his country" as a value opposed to interference with governmental power—this is freedom FROM external constraint, a rhetorical appeal to national liberty as a countervailing good against restrictions on state action.

sentence: For instance, the liberty of the press in this country, the freedom of debate in this House, is probably very injurious to Spain, and to every other despotic government;
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence invokes "liberty of the press" and "freedom of debate" — standard negative liberties naming freedom from interference with these activities — as causes opposed to despotic government; it reflects liberty as a value against tyranny.

sentence: I should hate this Government to be blamed for destroying what should be an integral part of any civilised society and of the policies of any party that values freedom of speech, freedom of thought and freedom of the written word.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker invokes freedom of speech, freedom of thought, and freedom of the written word as values to be protected from destruction by government interference—these are classic negative liberties from state intrusion.

sentence: But we were asked last night whether the Government are not at liberty to give advice to Foreign Powers;
haiku_v2_rationale: The Government's freedom to give advice to Foreign Powers is being asserted as a capacity or entitlement that the Government possesses; this describes empowerment/permission to act, not freedom from constraint.

sentence: I am glad to hear it is provided for, that some provision for religious liberty is made.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker expresses approval that provision for religious liberty—freedom from interference with religious practice and conscience—has been made.

sentence: Since the Union, Ireland has grievously declined, both in prosperity and political freedom.
haiku_v2_rationale: Ireland is said to have declined in political freedom—understood as freedom from external constraint or domination by the Union; the speaker asserts loss of freedom from interference in Irish self-governance.

sentence: Gentleman is saying that allowing people to advertise weapons in a way that incites violence is protecting freedom of speech.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom of speech—the freedom from interference with expression—is the object being discussed, even though the sentence presents it ironically as a justification for allowing harmful advertising.

sentence: asked the Minister of Food whether, in view of the fact that herring fishermen have sustained serious losses since the season opened in April and that the Herring Industry Board have been compelled to abandon their controlled schemes, he will restore complete freedom to the industry by withdrawing price controls.
haiku_v2_rationale: The herring industry is being freed from price controls and regulatory schemes—a removal of state interference with economic activity and commercial freedom.

sentence: They were of opinion that it would be more to the advantage and promotion of civil and religious liberty that the Church should be content with its present position, because, although it appeared by the Bill that the new bishops were not to have seats in the other House, yet he saw nothing in the Bill to prevent their being called up to the House of Lords at a future time.
haiku_v2_rationale: Civil and religious liberty is being understood here as freedom from interference by the State in the Church's affairs and the exercise of religion, with the speakers arguing that the Church accepting its position (limiting bishops' temporal power) better protects this non-interference.

sentence: I read an article last week in a Sunday newspaper in which it was said that there are no D Notices or Official Secrets Act in that heavenly country, in the land of freedom.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker contrasts a country free from state information controls (D Notices, Official Secrets Act) with one subject to such restrictions, invoking "the land of freedom" to mean freedom from government interference with information and knowledge.

sentence: no change except in the convictions of the Government, who are now willing to swallow all their old convictions, and to endeavour to impose still more binding fetters on the freedom of discussion in Committee of Supply.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence opposes imposing binding fetters on the freedom of discussion—fetters are constraints on freedom from interference with debate/discussion in parliamentary proceedings.

sentence: Schools have found that, having acquired that freedom, they want to keep it.
haiku_v2_rationale: Schools are freed to institutional autonomy and self-governance; they have acquired capacity to operate independently and want to retain that empowerment.

sentence: they have acknowledged from the first that we have entire fiscal liberty.
haiku_v2_rationale: This grants Ireland (or a jurisdiction) empowerment to govern its own fiscal/taxation matters — freedom to act autonomously in financial policy, not freedom from interference with pre-existing conduct.

sentence:
When he announced his proposals, he used these words:
 But I should warn industry generally that if there is any further breaking away, I shall consider myself at liberty to introduce legislation to restrict dividends in the next Finance Bill.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker claims freedom (capacity/empowerment) to introduce restrictive legislation—freedom from political constraint to act, an executive prerogative being asserted.

sentence: Trial by jury was the best security for the freedom of that house;
haiku_v2_rationale: The house (Parliament) is being secured in its freedom from interference with judicial proceedings and arbitrary control through the institutional protection of trial by jury.

sentence: One can imagine the freedom of discussion in the Cabinet.
haiku_v2_rationale: Cabinet members freed from interference with discussion; freedom of discussion is negative liberty—the absence of constraint on debate.

sentence: in reply, said, the noble Lord was at perfect liberty to apply to the Sheriff of Wigtonshire;
haiku_v2_rationale: The noble Lord was at perfect liberty to apply to the Sheriff — this is parliamentary procedure language, not a substantive claim about liberty as a value; "at liberty to" is an idiomatic expression meaning "permitted" or "able" in the formal procedural sense.

sentence: " and in other places he says it means "a certain freedom and independence conveyed by Royal Charter.
haiku_v2_rationale: A Royal Charter grants specific groups freedom and independence from external constraints—a non-interference right confirmed by royal authority.

sentence: That house was the guardian of the liberty and property of the people;
haiku_v2_rationale: The House (Parliament) is described as guardian/protector of liberty and property of the people — freedom from interference with those foundational rights against state tyranny.

sentence: The Bill violated the most fundamental principles of religious liberty.
haiku_v2_rationale: Religious liberty is freedom from interference with religious belief and practice—the sentence claims the Bill violates freedom from constraint on religion.

sentence: In the main, I have high regard for Liberty briefings, even though I sometimes I disagree with them, but this one is littered with unfounded assertions and important inaccuracies.
haiku_v2_rationale: "Liberty" here refers to the civil liberties organization as a proper noun (Liberty briefings), not a substantive claim about liberty-as-a-value or freedom from/to something.

sentence: He referred to freedom of contract.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom of contract is freedom from interference with the ability to enter into voluntary agreements; it names a constraint on what others may do to prevent contractual liberty.

sentence: We see its generous influence pervading the earlier labours of the Long Parliament, till that unfortunate conflict arose which, though it humbled the pride of kings, disgraced the cause of freedom, and closed the eventful scene in tyranny, in outrage, and in blood.
haiku_v2_rationale: The cause of freedom is invoked as a noble cause opposed to tyranny, outrage, and blood—a rhetorical invocation of liberty as what should be valued against oppression and despotism.

sentence: I say that this is as gross an interference with the freedom of the press as over occurred in Ireland, and that is saying a great deal.
haiku_v2_rationale: Freedom from interference with the press — a classic negative liberty against censorship or suppression of publication.

sentence: " But if the fire goes on it it not a question of thinking who is to blame, it is a question of all hands to assist, and because we love our country and believe in its future, because we believe in democracy and liberty, not only here but throughout the world, we say to the Government that if you are really going forward to put out the fire now we are bound to give you our help.
haiku_v2_rationale: The speaker invokes "liberty" as a value opposed to the dangers facing the country (implied tyranny or destruction), and pledges support to the Government to defend it—this is a rhetorical appeal to negative liberty (freedom from external threat/tyranny) as a cause uniting the nation.

sentence: I appreciate the recent announcements, but can the Minister reassure us that all universities will be able to access those loans, with freedom to invest in line with local priorities?
haiku_v2_rationale: Universities are being granted the capacity to invest in accordance with their own local priorities, rather than having investment decisions dictated externally—this is empowerment to self-determine within their domain.

sentence: When they are sold as prostitutes they are made to work in appalling conditions, with no freedom.
haiku_v2_rationale: Victims of trafficking are freed from control, coercion, and forced labour—negatively, freedom from enslavement and exploitation.

sentence: The letter written to the prisoner was as follows:—"Dear Sir, excuse the liberty I take in writing to you: I hope this letter may find yourself and Mrs.
haiku_v2_rationale: The phrase "the liberty I take in writing to you" is a polite rhetorical convention meaning "forgive my presumption in contacting you" — a procedural courtesy, not a substantive claim about freedom-as-a-value.

sentence:
If the noble marquis meant, that no measure having for its object to abridge the liberty of the subject ought ever to be adopted without their lordships
 138 
being put in possession of sufficient grounds to justify such a proceeding, he perfectly agreed with him in that proposition.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence concerns whether measures that abridge (restrict) the liberty/freedom of the subject should require justification; this addresses freedom from unwanted state interference with the subject's liberty.

sentence: The Economist believes that there should be bounds to the freedom of directors to do what they like.
haiku_v2_rationale: Directors are being freed from constraints on their discretionary action; the sentence advocates limiting this freedom by imposing bounds on their capacity to act without restriction.

sentence: It needs to be clearly understood in Departments, public bodies and elsewhere that if that freedom is found to be threatened, it will be protected.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence claims that unspecified freedom will be protected from threats (from interference/removal), without clarifying which specific liberty is at stake or whether the protection is about non-interference or empowerment.

sentence: If it is contrary to the policy of his Department to limit their freedom to engage labour in any way, why does the Department use the Fair Wages Clause, and why do they lay down conditions for local authorities who engage unemployed workers?
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence concerns "freedom to engage labour"—a freedom from interference with the capacity to hire workers without conditions or restrictions.

sentence: No country on earth has had so many minds, for such a series of years, engaged in such contemplations, and in attempts by every species of arrangement, moral, legal, and political, to increase the liberty and happiness of the people.
haiku_v2_rationale: The object of freedom is "liberty" itself; the sentence describes attempts through moral, legal, and political arrangements to increase liberty and happiness as a collective good — an enabling or empowering of the people's condition rather than simply freedom from interference.

sentence: Many controls are not restrictive but, rather, are productive of even greater freedom for other people.
haiku_v2_rationale: Controls are described as productive of freedom for other people—enabling people's capacity to act, not merely removing interference from them.

sentence: but, taking it altogether, the country enjoys as much practical liberty, and parliament is really as independent, as in any former reign.
haiku_v2_rationale: The country and Parliament enjoy freedom from interference with their independence and self-governance—they are unobstructed in their actual exercise of authority across reigns.

sentence: They are always serious and earnest in coming forward to expose their lives and fortunes in the just and necessary contest in which their country may be engaged, and when the constitution and liberty of the country are endangered.
haiku_v2_rationale: The constitution and liberty of the country are endangered—being preserved from threat/oppression. This is negative liberty: freedom of the nation from foreign threat and tyranny.

sentence: After all it should be remembered that liberty of discussion, even when it had amounted to license, bad resulted in making discussion profitable.
haiku_v2_rationale: The sentence invokes "liberty of discussion" — freedom from interference with debate and expression — as a value that produced beneficial social outcomes (making discussion profitable).
